name: Data
definition: Raw facts and information that can be processed.

Identify related concepts
Study examples and complexity
Practice with a small problem

name: Data Structure
definition: A method of organizing and storing data efficiently.

Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules

name: Linked List
definition: A linked list is a linear data structure made of nodes, where elements are stored non-contiguously in memory. Each node contains data and a pointer to the next node in the sequence.
step_by_step: Identify the Linked List use case
Choose the required operation such as Insert
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Singly Linked List
definition: Singly linked list is a linked list where each node points only to the next node in the sequence.
step_by_step: Identify the Singly Linked List use case
Choose the required operation such as Insert
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Doubly Linked List
definition: A linked list where each node contains pointers to both the next and previous nodes.
real_life_examples: Music playlist next and previous song navigation
Undo and redo functionality in software
step_by_step: Identify the Doubly Linked List use case
Choose the required operation such as Insert
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Circular Linked List
definition: A linked list where the last node points back to the first node, forming a circular structure.
step_by_step: Identify the Circular Linked List use case
Choose the required operation such as Insert
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Node
definition: A fundamental building block used in data structures such as linked lists, trees, and graphs, where each node stores data and one or more references to other nodes.
real_life_examples: People connected in a social network
step_by_step: Identify the Node use case
Choose the required operation such as Store Data
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules

name: Pointer
definition: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable.
step_by_step: Identify the Pointer use case
Choose the required operation such as Store Memory Address
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Dynamic Memory Allocation
definition: Dynamic memory allocation is the process of allocating memory for a program at runtime (while it is running), rather than at compile time. This allows for more flexible and efficient use of memory, as the program can request and release memory as needed.
real_life_examples: Managing memory for applications while they are running
Creating dynamic data structures like linked lists and trees
Operating systems allocating memory to processes
step_by_step: Identify the Dynamic Memory Allocation use case
Choose the required operation such as Allocate Memory
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Stack
definition: A linear data structure where operations are performed only at one end, the last element added to the stack is the first to be removed (LIFO: Last In First Out).
operations: push(x): Insert x at the top
IsEmpty(): Check if stack is empty
IsFull(): Check if stack is full
Size(): Check the size of the stack
step_by_step: Identify the Stack use case
Choose the required operation such as push(x)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Queue
definition: A linear structure where the first element added is the first to be removed (FIFO: First In First Out), elements are inserted at the rear and removed from the front.
operations: IsEmpty(): Check if queue is empty
IsFull(): Check if queue is full
Size(): Check the size of the queue
real_life_examples: Store customer lines, first come, first served
step_by_step: Identify the Queue use case
Choose the required operation such as Enqueue(x)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Circular Queue
definition: A queue where the rear wraps around to the front using circular indexing, efficiently reusing empty spaces created by dequeued elements.
operations: Enqueue(x): Enqueue, insert x at rear
IsEmpty(): Check if queue is empty
IsFull(): Check if queue is full
Size(): Check the size of the queue
step_by_step: Identify the Circular Queue use case
Choose the required operation such as Enqueue(x)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Priority Queue
definition: A queue where elements are removed based on priority instead of insertion order.
operations: push(x, p): Inserts new element x with priority p.
top(): Access the highest priority item
step_by_step: Identify the Priority Queue use case
Choose the required operation such as push(x, p)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Deque
definition: A double-ended queue that allows insertion and deletion at both the front and rear.
operations: push_front(x): Insert an element at the front end.
push_back(x): Insert an element at the rear end.
pop_front(): Delete an element from the front end.
pop_back(): Delete an element from the rear end.
getFront(): Retrieve (does not remove) the front element.
getRear(): Retrieve (does not remove) the rear element.
isEmpty(): Check if the deque is empty.
size(): Return the number of elements currently in the deque.
step_by_step: Identify the Deque use case
Choose the required operation such as push_front(x)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Recursion
definition: Recursion is a technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller subproblems.
real_life_examples: base_case_required : recursion stops when condition is met
step_by_step: Identify the Recursion use case
Choose the required operation such as Recursive Call
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Recursive Function
definition: A function that calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem.
real_life_examples: base_case_required : recursion stops when condition is met
step_by_step: Identify the Recursive Function use case
Choose the required operation such as Self Call
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Base Case
definition: The stopping condition in a recursive algorithm that prevents infinite recursion.
operations: CheckCondition(): Check if the input size is minimal enough to return a value
real_life_examples: A GPS stopping its search once the destination coordinates are reached
Walking down a staircase and stopping once our foot touches the ground floor
time_complexity_relation : T(n) depends on input size n
step_by_step: Identify the Base Case use case
Choose the required operation such as CheckCondition()
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Call Stack
definition: A stack used by programs to manage active function calls. Each time a function is called, it is added to the stack, executes its statements, and finally returns to where it was called from.
operations: push (Function Call): The program pushes a new frame into the stack when a function is called.
pop (Function Return): When a function finishes execution, the frame is popped off.
real_life_examples: Tracking active function calls in a program
step_by_step: Identify the Call Stack use case
Choose the required operation such as push (Function Call)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Sparse Matrix
definition: A matrix in which most of the elements are zero.
operations: insert(row, column, value): Adds a non-zero element to the matrix
delete(row, column): Removes a non-zero element.
transpose(): Reverse the row and column indices of all non-zero elements.
real_life_examples: matrix_dimension : A is m x n
step_by_step: Identify the Sparse Matrix use case
Choose the required operation such as insert(row, column, value)
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

name: Polynomial Representation
definition: A method of storing and manipulating polynomial expressions using arrays or linked lists.
operations: insert(coeff, exp): Add a new term to the polynomial
display(): Print the polynomial in a + bx^n format
add(poly1, poly2): Add two polynomials by summing coefficients of like terms
multiply(poly1, poly2): Multiply every term of poly1 by every term of poly2

time_complexity.Addition: O(n + m) // n, m are number of terms

step_by_step: Identify the Polynomial Representation use case
Choose the required operation such as create()
Apply the operation while maintaining the data structure rules
Analyze the time and space complexity

definition: A self-balancing binary search tree where the difference between heights of left and right subtrees cannot be more than one for any node.

name: Dynamic Programming
definition: An optimization technique that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing previous results.
real_life_examples: optimal_substructure : solution built from subproblem solutions
step_by_step: Define the subproblem
Find the recurrence
Set base cases
Compute results using memoization or tabulation
Return the final state

name: Greedy Algorithm
definition: An algorithmic strategy that makes the locally best choice at each step hoping to reach a globally optimal solution.
real_life_examples: Coin change with canonical coin systems
time_complexity_relation : T(n) depends on input size n
step_by_step: Sort or prioritize choices
Select the best available option
Check if the choice is valid
Update the solution
Repeat until complete

name: Backtracking
definition: A recursive problem-solving technique that builds candidates step by step and abandons a candidate when it cannot lead to a valid solution.
real_life_examples: time_complexity_relation : T(n) depends on input size n
step_by_step: Choose a candidate
Check whether it is valid
Recursively explore the next choice
Backtrack by undoing the choice
Continue until all solutions are found

name: Sliding Window
definition: A technique used to process contiguous subarrays or substrings efficiently by maintaining a moving window over the data.

name: String Matching
definition: A tree-like data structure used to store strings where each path represents a prefix. Memoization stores previously computed results so repeated calculations can be avoided. Tabulation is a bottom-up dynamic programming method that solves smaller subproblems first and stores their results in a table. String matching is the process of finding whether a pattern appears inside a text.
real_life_examples: search_complexity : O(L), where L is word length
step_by_step: Start from the root
For each character, move to or create a child node
Mark the end of a word
Search by following character links
Check if answer exists in cache
If yes, return cached result
If no, compute answer
Store answer in cache
Return result
Create a table
Set base cases
Fill table from smaller problems
Use final cell as answer
Choose a pattern
Compare pattern with text
Move to next position if not matched
Return match position if found

name: Adjacency List
definition: A graph representation where each vertex stores a list of its adjacent vertices. It is memory efficient for sparse graphs.

code_examples.cpp: vector<vector<int>> adj(V);\nadj[0].push_back(1);\nadj[1].push_back(0);

step_by_step: Create a list for each vertex
Store neighboring vertices
Add edges between connected vertices
Traverse neighbors when needed
Analyze graph complexity
